Name: RUsSelL FiGuErOa
chief_complaint: Teenage male with diabetes feeling unwell
hpi: A 16-year-old male with a history of Type 1 Diabetes presents to the ED complaining of generalized malaise, mild fatigue, and increased thirst for the past 24 hours. He denies fever, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or shortness of breath. Patient reports compliance with his insulin regimen, last dose taken this morning.
physician_note: 16 y.o. male with T1DM presenting with non-specific symptoms concerning for hyperglycemia or early DKA. Physical exam is benign with no signs of dehydration or respiratory distress. Differential includes mild hyperglycemia, viral illness, or early diabetic ketoacidosis. Plan: Check labs including CBC, CMP, VBG. Administer IV fluids and insulin per protocol if indicated by labs.

Name: mary roDRIGUez
chief_complaint: Severe shortness of breath and wheezing
hpi: 84-year-old female with a history of asthma presents to the ED with progressively worsening shortness of breath over the past 24 hours. She reports increased wheezing, a productive cough with yellow sputum, and subjective fever at home. She has been using her albuterol inhaler more frequently without significant relief.
physician_note: A: Elderly female with acute asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by a viral or bacterial respiratory infection. Differential includes pneumonia and acute decompensated heart failure. P: Initiate nebulized bronchodilators and systemic corticosteroids. Obtain CXR and send sputum for culture. Start broad-spectrum antibiotics given purulent sputum and age. Monitor respiratory status closely; consider BiPAP if no improvement.

scenario_hint: Hemorrhagic stroke: sudden severe headache + vomiting + altered mental status
chief_complaint: Sudden worst headache of my life, with vomiting
hpi: A 68-year-old male with a history of hypertension and on aspirin presents to the ED with sudden onset of the "worst headache of his life" approximately 2 hours prior to arrival. The headache is associated with projectile vomiting and rapidly progressing altered mental status. Family reports he became lethargic and disoriented and is now difficult to arouse.
physician_note: A&P: 68 y.o. male presenting with acute onset severe headache, vomiting, and altered mental status, highly concerning for hemorrhagic stroke, likely intraparenchymal hemorrhage given sudden onset and rapid decline. Differential includes subarachnoid hemorrhage, ischemic stroke with hemorrhagic conversion, or mass lesion. Plan: Emergent head CT, stat neurosurgery consult, obtain comprehensive labs including coagulation panel, initiate supportive care including airway protection if needed, strict blood pressure management.

Name: hARRy CLArk
chief_complaint: Feeling very weak and dizzy for two days
hpi: An 83-year-old female with a history of hypertension presents to the ED with a two-day history of increasing generalized weakness, fatigue, and intermittent dizziness. She reports feeling lightheaded when standing and has reduced oral intake. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, or focal neurological deficits. She has been taking paracetamol for body aches.
physician_note: 83 y/o female with HTN presenting with acute on chronic weakness and dizziness. Differential includes dehydration, orthostatic hypotension, cardiac arrhythmia, early sepsis (UTI), or acute coronary syndrome. Initial workup to include comprehensive metabolic panel, complete blood count, troponin, EKG, and urinalysis. Initiate IV fluids and closely monitor vital signs.

Name: chERYL CANnon
chief_complaint: Worst headache of my life, vision problems
hpi: A 44-year-old female with a history of hypertension presents to the ED with a sudden onset, severe headache described as the "worst of her life," associated with blurred vision for the past 2 hours. She attempted to relieve the pain with ibuprofen without success. She denies any preceding trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or focal neurological deficits.
physician_note: Patient presents with acute severe headache and visual disturbance, raising concern for hypertensive emergency, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Differential also includes severe migraine or atypical TIA. Plan: STAT head CT, comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, troponin, and EKG. Initiate IV antihypertensives to gradually lower blood pressure. Consult neurology if CT is abnormal.

Name: aMaNdA crOsS
chief_complaint: Bad asthma attack, can't breathe
hpi: 15-year-old female with a history of asthma presents to the ED with acute onset shortness of breath and wheezing, worsening over the past 4 hours. Patient reports using her albuterol inhaler every 20 minutes without significant relief. Denies fever, cough, or chest pain, but notes increased work of breathing. Last ED visit for asthma was 6 months ago.
physician_note: Patient presents with acute asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by a viral illness or environmental allergen. Differential includes bronchitis, foreign body aspiration. Exam reveals diffuse wheezing and increased work of breathing. Plan includes albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers, systemic corticosteroids, and close monitoring. Will consider CXR if no improvement.

scenario_hint: Peanut anaphylaxis: throat tightness, wheeze, hypotension
chief_complaint: Severe allergic reaction, can't breathe
hpi: A 31-year-old male with a history of asthma presents to the ED after accidentally ingesting peanuts approximately 30 minutes prior to arrival. He reports rapid onset of throat tightness, difficulty swallowing, and generalized itching. He also notes significant wheezing and feeling lightheaded, almost passing out. He has not taken any medications for this reaction prior to arrival.
physician_note: A&P: 31 y.o. male presenting with acute onset anaphylaxis secondary to peanut ingestion, manifesting with respiratory distress (wheezing, throat tightness) and signs of hypoperfusion (hypotension, lightheadedness). Differential includes severe asthma exacerbation, angioedema. Plan: Administer epinephrine IM immediately, initiate IV fluid bolus, administer IV corticosteroids and H1/H2 antihistamines. Continuous cardiac and respiratory monitoring is critical; prepare for potential airway intervention if respiratory status deteriorates. Labs drawn.

Name: david rosS
chief_complaint: Severe abdominal pain and fever
hpi: A 17-year-old male with a history of osteosarcoma undergoing chemotherapy presents to the ED with a 2-day history of worsening diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and subjective fever. He reports taking ibuprofen without significant relief. Parents are concerned about possible infection given his immunocompromised state.
physician_note: Pt is a young male with known cancer on chemotherapy presenting with acute abdominal pain and fever. Differential includes neutropenic enterocolitis, appendicitis, or other infectious etiologies. Plan is for broad-spectrum antibiotics, IVF, STAT labs including CBC with differential, CMP, lactate, blood cultures, and CT abdomen/pelvis. Oncology consult initiated.

Name: NiCOLe NOvaK
chief_complaint: Severe shortness of breath, can't catch my breath
hpi: A 67-year-old male with a history of asthma presents to the ED with progressively worsening shortness of breath, wheezing, and cough over the past 24 hours. He reports increased use of his rescue inhaler without significant relief. He denies fever or chills but feels very fatigued, experiencing significant respiratory distress.
physician_note: Patient presenting with acute asthma exacerbation, likely severe given current respiratory effort and hypoxia. Differential includes acute bronchitis, pneumonia, and possible cardiac etiology, though cardiac history is negative. Plan includes aggressive nebulized bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, oxygen supplementation, and chest X-ray to rule out consolidation or other pulmonary pathology. Will monitor respiratory status closely in a monitored setting.

Name: loRI TaYLoR
chief_complaint: Worsening right knee pain and swelling
hpi: 54-year-old male with known osteoarthritis presents with acute on chronic right knee pain, worsening over the past two days. Patient reports increased swelling and stiffness, limiting ambulation. Denies any preceding trauma, fever, chills, or redness of the joint.
physician_note: A: Right knee pain, likely an acute exacerbation of osteoarthritis. Differential includes gouty arthritis or a meniscal tear, with septic arthritis less likely given absence of systemic symptoms. P: Will obtain right knee X-rays to rule out acute fracture or significant effusion. Administer oral NSAIDs for pain control and recommend RICE therapy. Discharge home with follow-up instructed with PCP or orthopedics if symptoms persist or worsen.

Name: TaMMY oRTeGA
chief_complaint: Bad knee pain and feeling feverish
hpi: A 49-year-old male with a history of osteoarthritis presents with a 2-day history of worsening left knee pain, significantly more severe than his usual arthritis. He reports associated subjective fevers, chills, and generalized malaise. He denies any recent trauma or falls. Patient takes Paracetamol for his chronic arthritis.
physician_note: A 49M with known arthritis presenting with acute monoarticular pain and systemic symptoms. Differential includes septic arthritis, gout flare, or acute inflammatory arthritis exacerbation. Plan: Labs, knee x-ray, empiric antibiotics after joint aspiration if septic arthritis suspected, pain control, and rheumatology consult. Will monitor vital signs closely.

Name: maRy rIVera
chief_complaint: Severe shortness of breath and wheezing
hpi: A 31-year-old male with a history of asthma presents to the ED with acute worsening of dyspnea and wheezing for the past 24 hours. He reports increased cough and chest tightness, which has not improved with frequent albuterol inhaler use. Patient denies fever or recent illness but notes increased environmental allergens.
physician_note: Patient presenting with acute asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by environmental factors. Differential diagnosis includes viral bronchitis or allergic reaction. Plan: Administer nebulized albuterol/ipratropium, initiate systemic corticosteroids, obtain a chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia, and monitor respiratory status closely. Consider magnesium sulfate if no significant improvement.

Name: rIckeY HerNanDEZ
chief_complaint: Severe stomach pain and feeling weak
hpi: 52-year-old female with a history of osteoarthritis presents to the ED with acute onset, severe epigastric pain for the past 12 hours. The pain is described as burning and radiates to her back, associated with nausea, one episode of non-bloody emesis, and generalized weakness. She reports increased use of over-the-counter NSAIDs for her arthritis pain in the last week.
physician_note: 52F presenting with acute epigastric pain, weakness, and nausea. Differential includes acute gastritis, peptic ulcer disease (PUD) possibly complicated by NSAID use, pancreatitis, and cardiac ischemia given her age and associated symptoms. Plan is to initiate IV fluids, administer antiemetics and a PPI, obtain EKG, CBC, CMP, lipase, troponin, and lactate. Monitor for signs of GI bleeding or cardiac compromise and consider imaging such as abdominal ultrasound if pain persists or localizes.

Name: DOuglaS PeNa
chief_complaint: Generalized weakness and feeling unwell
hpi: Patient is an 84-year-old female with a history of obesity, presenting with a 3-day history of progressive generalized weakness and fatigue. She reports feeling lightheaded intermittently but denies syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath, or fever. She states she has been eating and drinking less due to lack of energy. Currently on Aspirin daily.
physician_note: 84F with generalized weakness, likely multifactorial, potentially due to dehydration or mild deconditioning. Differentials include anemia, early infection, or electrolyte imbalance. Plan: IV fluids, CBC, BMP, UA. Will monitor symptoms and vitals. Discharge home if labs and clinical picture allow.

Name: debORaH wArREN DdS
chief_complaint: Weakness and fatigue for two days
hpi: Patient is a 75-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes presenting with generalized weakness and profound fatigue for the past two days. He reports decreased oral intake and some lightheadedness upon standing. Denies fever, chest pain, or focal neurological deficits.
physician_note: A/P: 75 y/o male with T2DM presenting with generalized weakness. Vitals are stable, and initial labs are reassuring, though glucose is mildly elevated. Differential includes dehydration, mild infection, or suboptimally controlled diabetes. Plan: IV fluids, close monitoring of blood glucose, and further workup as indicated.

Name: paTricK hEbeRT
chief_complaint: Severe headache, blurry vision, and chest pressure
hpi: Patient is a 44-year-old male with a history of hypertension on Lipitor, presenting with acute onset severe headache, blurred vision, and chest pressure for the past 4 hours. He denies fever, cough, or recent trauma. Patient reports compliance with Lipitor but admits to missing his blood pressure medication for several days. He has no prior history of similar symptoms.
physician_note: 44 y/o male with HTN presents with acute headache, blurred vision, and chest pressure. Vitals notable for BP 180/105. No focal neurological deficits or signs of acute end-organ damage on initial assessment. Differential includes hypertensive urgency, migraine with aura, and atypical angina. Plan: Administer oral labetalol, obtain EKG, CBC, CMP, Troponin, and close BP monitoring; will reassess for improvement and stability.

Name: mORgAn biRd
chief_complaint: Sudden, severe right knee pain and swelling
hpi: A 44-year-old male with a history of arthritis presents to the ED with abrupt onset of excruciating right knee pain and associated swelling for the past 6 hours. He denies any recent trauma or fall. Pain is 8/10, constant, and exacerbated by movement. He notes some subjective warmth and redness around the joint, but no fever or chills. He takes Lipitor for hyperlipidemia.
physician_note: Patient presents with acute monoarticular arthritis. Differential includes acute gouty flare, pseudogout, or septic arthritis. Given stable vitals and normal initial labs, septic arthritis is less likely but not completely ruled out without joint fluid analysis. Plan includes rest, ice, NSAIDs, and consideration for joint aspiration if effusion is significant, along with orthopedic consult for definitive management.

scenario_hint: 8-month-old female with high fever 39.8C, lethargy, poor feeding — pediatric red flag, always escalate
chief_complaint: Baby with high fever, very sleepy, not eating well
hpi: An 8-month-old female with a history of asthma presents to the ED with a 2-day history of fever, lethargy, and decreased oral intake. Parents report temperatures up to 103.6F (39.8C) and significantly reduced activity and responsiveness. She has had poor feeding, only taking small amounts of formula, and is not producing as many wet diapers. Parents attempted acetaminophen at home with minimal relief.
physician_note: 8mo F with high fever, lethargy, and poor feeding concerning for sepsis or severe viral illness, potentially complicated by underlying asthma. Differential includes bacterial meningitis, severe bronchiolitis, or pyelonephritis. Plan includes immediate IV access, fluid bolus, pan-culture (blood, urine, CSF if indicated), broad-spectrum antibiotics (Ceftriaxone, Vancomycin), respiratory support as needed, and continuous monitoring. Will consult pediatrics.

Name: eliZABetH tanNer
chief_complaint: Generalized weakness and fatigue for a few days
hpi: A 61-year-old female with a history of metastatic breast cancer presents to the ED complaining of increasing generalized weakness and fatigue over the past 3 days. She denies fever, chills, chest pain, or shortness of breath. She reports adherence to her current chemotherapy regimen and has been taking paracetamol for mild body aches.
physician_note: Patient presents with non-specific symptoms of weakness and fatigue, common in cancer patients undergoing treatment. Vitals are stable, and initial labs are within normal limits per chart review. Differential diagnosis includes chemotherapy-related fatigue, mild anemia, or electrolyte imbalance. Plan is to complete a full workup including CBC, CMP, and discuss with oncology for outpatient management if stable for discharge.

Name: Amanda cHAPMaN
chief_complaint: Severe stomach ache and throwing up
hpi: A 27-year-old obese female presents to the ED with acute onset, severe epigastric pain that has migrated to the right upper quadrant over the past 8 hours. She reports nausea, multiple episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis, and subjective fevers. She takes daily aspirin for unknown reasons.
physician_note: 27F presenting with classic symptoms of acute cholecystitis. Other considerations include gastritis, pancreatitis, or hepatitis. Initial plan includes IV fluids, antiemetics, analgesia, CBC, CMP, lipase, and a right upper quadrant ultrasound. Given abnormal test result, anticipate leukocytosis and possibly elevated LFTs.

Name: davID WaRD
chief_complaint: Mild chest discomfort, intermittent
hpi: Patient is a 59-year-old male presenting with mild, intermittent chest discomfort for the past 24 hours. He describes the pain as a pressure sensation, 3/10 in severity, non-radiating, and relieved with rest. He denies any associated shortness of breath, palpitations, diaphoresis, or nausea. Patient has a history of obesity and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes Lipitor.
physician_note: A&P: 59M presenting with atypical chest pain. Differential includes musculoskeletal pain, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and less likely, stable angina given the patient's stable presentation and lack of acute symptoms. Plan: Obtain ECG, order cardiac enzymes including troponin, and a basic metabolic panel. Will monitor patient's symptoms and vital signs closely. If studies are unremarkable and symptoms resolve, patient will be discharged with return precautions and outpatient follow-up.

Name: JILL huErTa
chief_complaint: Sudden onset of chest pressure and shortness of breath
hpi: A 64-year-old female with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden onset substernal chest pressure radiating to her left arm, starting approximately 2 hours prior to arrival. She describes the pain as a heavy sensation, associated with dyspnea and diaphoresis. She denies recent trauma, fever, or cough.
physician_note: Assessment: 64F with acute chest pain concerning for Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) vs. anxiety vs. esophageal spasm. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, no acute ST changes. Initial troponin is elevated. Plan: Administer aspirin, nitroglycerin, and obtain serial troponins and ECGs. Consult Cardiology for further management and consider admission for NSTEMI.
